critic_raw:
The splitting strategy divides data by weekly time windows rather than by patient ID. Diabetic retinopathy datasets routinely contain multiple images per patient (bilateral imaging, follow-up visits). A patient whose left eye image appears in training and right eye image appears in test introduces patient-level leakage — the model learns patient-specific vascular features and applies them during evaluation. This inflates test performance beyond what would be achievable on truly new patients.

Preprocessing normalization is fit on the entire dataset before splitting (explicitly stated). Image standardization parameters incorporate test-set pixel statistics, which constitutes distribution leakage.

The comparison pits CNN predictions against consensus expert grading using the same consensus labels as ground truth. The expert grading IS the ground truth — comparing the CNN against the process that defines correctness creates a circular evaluation. The 'baseline' has definitionally perfect agreement with itself on the exact labels it produced. The experiment should be designed to compare on an independent reference standard (e.g., optic coherence tomography or clinical outcome), not against the labeling process.

The severe class has approximately 250 images out of 5,000 total. Fine-tuning EfficientNet-B3 (12 million parameters) on 250 severe cases even with class weighting is severely underpowered. The model is likely to have high variance on the severe class, but macro-AUROC averaging will mask this by being dominated by the no-retinopathy class performance.

The 'comparison' to consensus expert grading is methodologically misaligned: experts produce a single-label grading decision, while the CNN produces continuous probability outputs evaluated by AUROC across four one-vs-rest thresholds. Experts are not evaluated by AUROC — their performance is evaluated by the same kappa metric reported for inter-rater reliability. The comparison uses different evaluation protocols for the two systems.
all_issues_raised: Data is split by weekly time window rather than patient ID — patients with bilateral images or multiple visits will have images distributed across train and test sets, causing patient-level leakage where the model learns individual retinal vascular signatures.
Preprocessing normalization and standardization parameters are fit on the entire dataset before splitting, meaning test-set pixel intensity statistics influence training transformations.

The baseline is expert consensus grading, which is also the ground truth labeling process — comparing CNN predictions to the labeling system that defines correctness is circular and does not constitute a valid performance comparison against an independent standard.

The severe retinopathy class contains approximately 250 images — fine-tuning EfficientNet-B3 on this few examples with high-dimensional inputs is severely underpowered, and macro-AUROC averaging masks per-class severity.

Expert performance is measured by kappa (categorical agreement) while CNN performance is measured by AUROC (continuous discrimination) — these are not comparable evaluation protocols, making the head-to-head claim methodologically incoherent.

The baseline is expert consensus grading, which is also the ground truth labeling process — comparing CNN predictions to the labeling system that defines correctness is circular and does not constitute a valid performance comparison against an independent standard.

Expert performance is measured by kappa (categorical agreement) while CNN performance is measured by AUROC (continuous discrimination) — these are not comparable evaluation protocols, making the head-to-head claim methodologically incoherent.
